text: This video will demonstrate the important concepts, key steps and fundamental anatomical considerations in laparoscopic ventral mesh rectopexy in a female patient who has previously undergone mesh hysteropexy.
This case is of an otherwise healthy, forty-eight year old female who has previously undergone sacrohysteropexy for uterine prolapse.
She presents with mixed symptoms of obstructed defecation and urge fecal incontinence.
Defecating proctography, anorectal manometry, ultrasound, and examination under anesthesia were used to investigate her symptoms.
At the time of preoperative proctography, the patient could not evacuate the contrast agent from the rectum.
This was associated with an excessive perineal descent.
Examination under anaesthesia was subsequently performed to exclude an internal rectal prolapse.

The surgeon uses a circular anal dilator device to visually assess for internal rectal prolapse, examining the rectal tissue for signs of prolapse into the level of an existing rectocele or beyond the anal sphincter and canal.
These select pictures are an example of the findings from this patient's EUA.
which demonstrated a grade IV internal rectoanal interception, the highest grade of internal rectal prolapse according to the Oxford prolapse grading system.
The pathological descent of the rectal wall into the anal canal that defines a grade IV prolapse is demonstrated diagrammatically here.

Four trocars are inserted into the abdominal wall for the rectopexy procedure, providing access for surgical instruments and visualization, although mesh rectopexy typically requires only three ports.
An additional port is placed on the left side of the abdomen to accommodate anticipated difficulties from a previous hysteropexy during the rectopexy procedure.
We begin with three simple steps to maximize pelvic exposure.
The first of these is achieved preoperatively by positioning the patient in a steep Trendelenburg.
Second, with fixation of the uterus to the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon fixes the sigmoid colon to the anterolateral abdominal wall using one or more epiploic appendages.
A number of anatomical landmarks are well demonstrated in the snapshot.
The sacral promontory is clearly visualized, with the peritoneum tented towards it by mesh from a previous hysteropexy.
The right ureter is visible, positioned laterally to the right common iliac artery and its bifurcation.
The pouch of Douglas is clearly visualized without active traction, benefiting from the maximal exposure achieved through previous steps.
A large right ovarian cyst is visible, presenting a technical challenge during the rectopexy procedure.
Before proceeding further, it is important to review the finished product of sacro-hystereopexy and what can be expected during dissection.
This diagrammatic representation demonstrates how the uterus becomes suspended by its attachment to the promontory.
The surgeon secures mesh circumferentially around the cervix and extends a connected arm of mesh toward the sacral promontory for attachment.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh, concealing it beneath the peritoneal layer.
The video shows the peritoneum tented by the mesh extending from the cervix to the sacral promontory, with a previous tack visible through the peritoneum.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum to expose the sacral promontory, initiating the dissection process.
The surgeon releases the mesh from a prior hysteropexy to expose the sacral promontory and access the lateral aspect of the rectum.
The surgeon continues the dissection medially to access the rectovaginal space, carefully navigating between the rectum and vagina.
The surgeon continues dissection down to the pelvic floor, revealing the anatomical structures and tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon limits dissection to the rectovaginal space during ventral rectopexy, preserving the autonomic nerves located in the posterior rectal space.
The surgeon performs a digital examination of the rectum to aid in the dissection down to the rectopelvic junction, ensuring precise navigation and exposure of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon places the mesh into the peritoneum and introduces PDS suture material through five millimeter cannulas from Applied Medical.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the anterior rectal wall, taking superficial bites that include the seromuscular layers of the rectum while leaving the mucosa intact.

The mesh used in this case is a surgesis biological mesh, which has the added advantages of being resistant to infection and promoting complete remodeling so that the mesh becomes incorporated into surrounding tissue whilst maintaining long-term strength.
The surgeon places four additional sutures along the lateral aspects of the rectum and one suture medially, securing the mesh circumferentially to the rectal wall.
The surgeon pulls the mesh towards the sacral promontory, visually repositioning the rectum to its original anatomical alignment.
The surgeon carefully adjusts the mesh placement to avoid excessive tension and prevent the inadvertent opening of the anorectal angle, ensuring the preservation of continence.
The peritoneum overlying the sacral promontory is shown incised and dissected, revealing the underlying anatomical structures at the beginning of the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon palpates the sacral promontory before inserting a tack into the presacral fascia overlying the promontory to secure the mesh.
A tack placed into the peritoneum causes visible bleeding and demonstrates a lack of tensile strength, as the fascia is not properly incorporated into the tack.
The surgeon removes the previously placed tack and palpates the sacral promontory once more.
The surgeon places another tack into the presacral fascia overlying the sacral promontory.
The surgeon releases mesh previously attached to the sacral promontory from a prior hysteropexy, facilitating further dissection in the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon sutures the mesh together to preserve the original repair, ensuring it is securely attached.
The surgeon sutures the intact mesh to the presacral fascia, reinforcing the integrity of the original repair.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the underlying mesh, completing the ventral rectopexy and preventing adhesion of the mesh to the small bowel and surrounding structures.
The patient had an excellent post-operative outcome, with initial complaints of incomplete emptying and urge fecal incontinence having completely resolved.
She was opening her bowels twice daily to soft stool with no excessive straining.
Post-operative digital examination in the clinic confirmed appropriate positioning of the mesh one centimetre above the puborectal sling.
